brand.title: Allocation Studio
brand.tagline: Multi-level demand forecasting + agentic decisions for retail supply chains
brand.demo: Synthetic-data demo · built with Claude Code

start.title: Configure the Allocation Studio
start.subtitle: Same engine, different store. Pick the kind of business you'd like to see the demo run on, and the language you want the interface in. You can change either later.
start.sectorHelp: Each pick swaps in a sector-specific catalog (~18–25 SKUs), categories and seasonal patterns. Locations and KPIs stay the same.
start.localeHelp: All copy, KPI names and tooltips are localised.
start.note: Synthetic data, deterministic from a seed. Nothing is sent or stored.

missed.title: Missed sales opportunities
missed.subtitle: Recent lost sales — what caused them, how to recover, how to stop the bleed
missed.framework: Diagnose → Recover → Prevent. Click any row to expand.

decisions.title: Today's priority decisions
decisions.subtitle: {count} pending · {impact} of weekly impact at stake
decisions.empty: All caught up. New signals will appear here as the week progresses.

info.stockHealth: % of SKU-store pairs with healthy cover (2–8 weeks). Below 60% typically means too many outliers — too thin or too deep.
info.sellThrough: Last week's sales ÷ (sales + remaining stock). Higher = stock is moving faster. Below 2% often signals slow-moving SKUs or overstock.
info.stockOuts: SKU-locations that ran to zero in the last week while demand was still positive. Each one is a lost sale today and a credibility issue with the store team.
info.weeklyRevenue: Units sold last completed week × list price. Use the vs LY pill to check whether growth is organic or driven by campaigns.
info.weeklyMargin: Units × (price − COGS). Margin is what you actually defend in the allocation meeting, not revenue.
info.inTransit: Units already committed on open POs (supplier → DC and DC → store). Always check this before approving more transfers.
info.forecastMape: Mean Absolute Percent Error over the last 8 weeks. Under 12% is solid; above 20% means forecasts are driving noise into decisions.
info.missedRevenue: Estimated lost sales from full stock-outs plus size gaps, valued at list price. The number this role is hired to reduce.
info.imbalanceCost: Weekly carrying cost of the excess units sitting at over-covered stores while others run thin. Redistributing cuts this directly.
info.salesByRegion: Weekly units sold per region, last 26 weeks. Look for divergence — a regional spike vs others usually maps to a campaign week.
info.stockByRegion: Average weeks of cover across SKUs per region. Under 2w is thin; over 10w means capital is sitting idle.
info.topMovers: SKUs with the highest revenue last completed week. Protect their size curve first — they are where the sell-through comes from.
info.atRisk: SKU-locations with less than 2 weeks of cover and positive demand. Replenish or redirect in the next cycle.
info.missedOpportunities: Top stock-outs and size gaps by lost revenue. Size-gap tags show which exact sizes went to zero.
info.imbalances: SKUs where one store has thin cover and another is long — the lowest-cover vs highest-cover pairing per SKU, with the carrying cost.
info.allocation: Recommendations triggered by live demand signals (velocity, upcoming campaign, size gap), not by the pre-season plan. Each one is monetised before approval.
info.sizesHeatmap: Rows = stores, columns = sizes. Red = stock-out with demand, amber = thin size cover, green = healthy. Top bar shows expected demand share per size.
info.forecastChart: Solid line = actual sales, dashed line = forecast, shaded band = 80% confidence, dotted grey = last year, orange bars = marketing spend (right axis).
info.risks: Risks tab: SKU-locations predicted to stock out in 2/4/6 weeks with revenue at risk. Opportunities tab: overstock pockets that can fund redistributions.
info.categories: Performance by product category — the rollup you bring into the Merchandise Planning meeting.
info.decisionsQueue: Top open decisions ranked by absolute revenue impact across stock-outs, campaign signals, size gaps, imbalances and overstock. Approve, defer or escalate inline.
info.products: Active SKUs with global stock, weeks-of-cover (traffic-light) and a per-product size grid. Red bars in the size grid mark sizes that are out of stock somewhere.
info.simulator: What-if sliders. Move marketing spend, lead time and target cover; the four KPI cards on the right recompute live so you can defend the trade-off before approving a campaign-driven transfer.
info.storeDetail: Store-level execution view. Tile colours show cover health, the SKU table flags size gaps, and the right column lists incoming POs.
info.forecastCompare: Backtested MAPE per horizon for every method. Lower is better. The champion is the model the supply chain runs on today; challengers are TSFMs being evaluated for promotion.
info.hierarchical: Bottom-up sums multiplied from store level vs MinT-reconciled values. Δ above ±3% means the hierarchy disagrees and a reconciliation review is triggered.
info.labPage: Engineering side of the forecast — model registry, training schedule, drift detection and agentic auto-experiments.
info.modelRegistry: Every model currently registered with backtested MAPE and the gap to the legacy baseline (Prophet). Champion runs in production; challengers are auto-promoted when they beat the champion for 3 consecutive runs.
info.drift: Series whose recent pattern (last 4 weeks) diverges from the training distribution. Score above 0.7 triggers an automatic retrain; above 0.85 routes the series to a TSFM (Chronos / TimesFM) which handles regime shifts better.
info.pipelineRuns: Each run trains 5–6 models on fresh data, backtests them and records MAPE Δ vs the previous run. Runs are scheduled, drift-triggered, manual or agentic (an LLM agent proposes the next experiment based on the registry state).

forecastLab.pageSubtitle: Multi-model demand forecasting with Time Series Foundation Models, classical baselines and an ensemble champion. Backtest by horizon, reconcile across the hierarchy.
forecastLab.compareSubtitle: Backtested MAPE per horizon, sector-wide. The champion is the model the supply chain runs on today.
forecastLab.hierSubtitle: Bottom-up sums against MinT-style reconciled forecasts. Δ shows where the hierarchy disagrees with itself.
forecastLab.hierFooter: Reconciled values use minimum-trace (MinT) reconciliation across the network → region → store hierarchy. Δ above ±3% triggers a reconciliation review.

lab.title: ML Lab
lab.subtitle: Model registry, training pipelines and drift watch — the engineering side of the forecast.
lab.registrySubtitle: All models in production, with backtested MAPE and the gap to the legacy baseline.
lab.driftSubtitle: Series whose recent pattern diverges from training data — sorted by drift score.
lab.runsSubtitle: Daily training schedule plus drift-triggered and agentic auto-experiments.

about.title: About this demo
about.subtitle: A portfolio project by Gemma Garcia de la Fuente for the Senior Retail Allocator role at On
about.demoIntro: Everything on this site is synthetic data rendered from deterministic seeds. It is a self-initiated prototype of how a modern allocation console could feel — combining demand-led replenishment, missed-sales detection, size performance and stock-imbalance analysis. Not affiliated with On AG.
about.hobbiesCopy: When I'm not building allocation tools, you'll find me on ski slopes through winter, on mountain trails through summer, and most weekends patiently teaching our recently-adopted pup that the Limmat riverside is for walking — she firmly disagrees with the ban on sniffing every flower along the way.

allocation.title: Demand-led allocation
allocation.subtitle: Replenishment recommendations driven by observed sell-through and store-level demand signals — not a monolithic season forecast
allocation.methodology: Each recommendation is triggered by a live demand signal (velocity, upcoming campaign, size gap) rather than the pre-season plan.

footer.disclaimer: Portfolio demo of an AI-assisted allocation tool. All data — products, locations, sales, inventory — is synthetic and deterministically generated from a seed. Brand and product names are placeholders chosen to evoke a realistic catalog and are © their respective owners; this site is not affiliated with any of them.
footer.stack: Built with Next.js, next-intl, Recharts and Tailwind
